name: Tinder
overview:
Tinder is a location-based dating app where users swipe right (like) or left (pass) on profiles. When two users
both swipe right, it's a match and they can chat. The system must serve a personalized deck of nearby profiles,
process swipes in real-time, detect mutual matches instantly, and support messaging between matched users.
The recommendation system must balance proximity, user preferences, and engagement signals.

inScope: Serve a deck of nearby profiles to swipe on
Detect mutual matches and notify both users

items: Profile deck served in <200ms; swipe processing <100ms
Match detection must be correct — false matches or missed matches are unacceptable
99.9% availability; brief deck staleness (12-hour refresh) is acceptable
Eventual consistency for deck freshness; strong consistency for match creation
Location privacy: user coordinates must never be exposed exactly — only approximate distances

entities.name: Profile
Swipe
Match

endpoints.response: { profiles: [{ user_id, name, age, photos, distance_km }] }

approaches.title: SQL Query with PostGIS
approaches.description: Query PostgreSQL with PostGIS for users within radius, filtered by age and gender preferences. At 50M DAU each requesting 20 profiles, this is 1B DB queries/day. PostGIS radius queries on 50M users are slow without careful indexing.
approaches.tradeoff: Too slow at scale; DB cannot handle query volume.

approaches.title: Redis Geo + Profile Cache
approaches.description: Store user locations in Redis Geo. GEORADIUS returns nearby users. Filter by preferences in application layer. Fast proximity queries. But filtering 10k nearby users by age/gender in application layer is wasteful.
approaches.tradeoff: Application-layer filtering is wasteful; returns many irrelevant profiles.

approaches.title: Pre-Computed Recommendation Deck
approaches.description: A recommendation engine pre-computes a deck of 200 candidate profiles for each active user every 12 hours. The deck is stored in Redis (key = user_id, value = sorted list of candidate user_ids). The deck considers: proximity (Redis Geo), age/gender preferences, ELO-style attractiveness score, and already-swiped profiles (excluded). At swipe time, the next profile is popped from the deck. When the deck is exhausted, a new one is computed.
approaches.tradeoff: Pre-computed deck may become stale (users move, new users join); 12-hour refresh may

approaches.title: miss new users.
approaches.description: Match Detection Query DB for Mutual Like When user A likes user B, query the DB to check if user B has already liked user A. Simple but requires a DB query on every like. At 1B swipes/day, this is 1B DB queries/day.

approaches.title: Redis Bloom Filter for Mutual Like Detection
approaches.description: When user A likes user B, check a Redis Bloom filter: 'has user B liked user A?' If the Bloom filter says yes (with possible false positives), query the DB to confirm. If confirmed, create a match. The Bloom filter is populated when likes are recorded. False positive rate ~0.1% means 0.1% of non-matches trigger a DB query — acceptable.
approaches.tradeoff: False positives cause unnecessary DB queries; Bloom filter must be maintained per user.

components.name: Recommendation Service
components.responsibility: Pre-computes profile decks; uses Redis Geo + ELO score + preference filters

components.name: Swipe Service
components.responsibility: Processes swipes; checks Bloom filter for mutual likes; creates matches

components.name: Match Service
components.responsibility: Creates match records; sends push notifications to both users

components.name: Messaging Service
components.responsibility: Handles in-app chat between matched users; uses WebSocket

components.name: Profile Service
components.responsibility: Manages user profiles and photos; serves profile data

components.name: Cassandra
components.responsibility: Stores swipes and matches; partition key = user_id

questions.title: 2. Swipe Deck Freshness
questions.content:
The pre-computed deck is refreshed every 12 hours. To handle new users and location changes, the
Recommendation Service also maintains a 'hot deck' of users who joined or moved in the last hour. When a
user requests their next profile, the hot deck is checked first. This ensures new users appear in decks quickly
without waiting for the full 12-hour refresh.

questions.title: 3. Messaging Architecture
questions.content:
Tinder's messaging uses a WebSocket-based chat system. Messages are stored in Cassandra (partition key =
match_id, clustering key = timestamp). When a message is sent, it's published to Kafka. The Messaging
Service consumes Kafka and pushes the message to the recipient's WebSocket connection. If the recipient is
offline, a push notification is sent via APNs/FCM.

questions.title: 4. Photo Storage and Delivery
questions.content:
Profile photos are stored in S3 and served via CloudFront CDN. Photos are resized to multiple dimensions
(thumbnail 100x100, card 400x400, full 1080x1080) by a Lambda function triggered on upload. The CDN
caches photos at edge nodes. Profile photos are the dominant bandwidth cost — CDN caching is critical for
cost efficiency.

questions.title: 5. Privacy and Safety
questions.content:
Tinder implements several privacy features: (1) Location fuzzing: user locations are shown as approximate
distances (not exact coordinates), (2) Block/report: blocked users are excluded from each other's decks, (3)
Photo verification: AI-based photo verification to prevent catfishing, (4) Incognito mode: users can browse
without appearing in others' decks. Blocked users are stored in a Redis set per user for fast exclusion during
deck computation.

keyFlows: ① GET /recs → Rec Svc → pop from Redis pre-computed deck
② POST /swipe (LIKE) → Swipe Svc → check Redis Bloom filter
③ Bloom filter hit → DB confirm → Match Svc creates match
④ Match notification → WebSocket push to both users
⑤ Matched users chat via Messaging Svc (WebSocket + Cassandra)

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study the ELO rating system: how it was originally designed for chess, how Tinder adapted it
for profile ranking, and the mathematical properties that make it self-balancing.
Expert Note: Learn about Bloom filters: the mathematical relationship between false positive rate, number
of hash functions, and filter size, and how to implement a per-user Bloom filter in Redis.
Expert Note: Understand location privacy in dating apps: how to fuzz GPS coordinates to prevent exact
location disclosure while maintaining useful proximity information.
Expert Note: Study WebSocket-based messaging at scale: how to handle message ordering, delivery
guarantees, and offline message delivery in a distributed chat system.
Expert Note: Learn about recommendation system cold-start: how to handle new users with no swipe
history, and how to bootstrap their ELO score and preference model.
